---
title: "One-Dimensional Minimization: the Derivative Test"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE  = "#2b3a67"    # the objective f
TANGENT = "#c1121f"   # tangent lines / the optimum
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / stationary points
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article of the Optimization
> track and the place where the machinery actually starts. It assumes only
> [OPTM-00 *What Is an Optimization Problem?*], which fixed the vocabulary — the
> **decision variable** $x$, the **objective** $f$, the **feasible set**, and the
> statement $\min_{x} f(x)$ — and drew the difference between a *global* and a *local*
> minimum. Here we answer the very first concrete question that vocabulary raises:
> for a function of a *single* variable, exactly how does calculus find a minimum, and
> when does that calculus quietly lie to us? Everything we do uses nothing above the
> **Calculus-I floor** (limits, the derivative, the chain rule, the
> second-derivative test) — we lean on it, we do not rebuild it. It sets up
> [OPTM-02 *Minimizing in Many Variables*], which is this exact logic with the single
> derivative replaced by the gradient.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

We are handed a function $f$ of one real variable and asked for the input that makes it
smallest. This article derives the answer that every later optimizer is a generalization
of. The **first-order condition** $f'(x)=0$ falls out of one observation: if the slope
is not zero, you can still step downhill, so you were not at the bottom. The
**second-derivative test** $f''(x)>0$ then sorts the flat spots into minima, maxima, and
the genuinely ambiguous $f''=0$ case (which we resolve honestly, with $x^4$ as the
witness). We carry the method through three real objectives — a quadratic cost, a
Poisson negative log-likelihood, and a constrained inventory cost whose true minimum
sits at a *boundary where the derivative is not zero* — the honest case where the
derivative test alone gives the wrong answer. Finally, when $f'(x)=0$ cannot be solved in
closed form, we introduce **bisection** and **golden-section search**: the first
*iterative* descent in the series, and the seed of the whole gradient-descent story to
come.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
At an interior minimum of a smooth one-variable function, the tangent line is **flat**:
$f'(x^\star)=0$. This is *necessary* — a nonzero slope always points downhill in some
direction, so you can do better. It is **not sufficient**: a flat tangent also marks
maxima and inflection points. The **second derivative** disambiguates — $f''(x^\star)>0$
(curving upward) confirms a minimum. And the entire test can still **miss the answer**
when the minimum lives at a *boundary* of the feasible set, where the slope need not
vanish. Know all three clauses, and you know exactly when "set the derivative to zero"
is the whole story and when it is a trap.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The single equation $f'(x)=0$ will look like a rule you
were handed in Calc 1, but by the end you should see it as *forced*: it is the precise
mathematical statement of "there is no downhill direction left." The three failure clauses
— sufficiency, the $f''=0$ tie, and boundary minima — are not footnotes; they are where
practitioners get burned, and they are the reason the rest of the Optimization track
exists.

# Why we are here: the problem that forces the question {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what does it actually mean to be "at
the bottom," and how would calculus ever know?** Every step answers a piece of that.

## The setting, inherited from OPTM-00

[OPTM-00] set up the object. We have a real-valued **objective** $f$ and we want

$$
x^\star = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{x \in C} f(x),
$$ {#eq-argmin}

the *location* (not the value) of the smallest output, where $C$ is the **feasible set**
of allowed inputs. For most of this article $C$ is an interval of the real line — often
all of $\mathbb{R}$ — and $f$ is smooth (it has as many derivatives as we need). The
question of *when a minimizer even exists* was OPTM-00's job; here we assume one exists
and ask **how to find it**.

This is not an idle exercise. Almost every model in the rest of this series is fit by
minimizing a one-number score over its parameters: a regression minimizes squared error,
a classifier minimizes a loss, a likelihood-based method minimizes a *negative*
log-likelihood. When that score depends on a single parameter — a single rate, a single
threshold, a single step length — we are in exactly the one-dimensional world of this
article. And even when there are many parameters, the workhorse algorithms (gradient
descent, coordinate descent, line search) repeatedly solve a *one-dimensional*
subproblem: "how far do I step in this direction?" So the 1-D case is not a warm-up we
discard. It is the atom that the multivariate machinery is built out of.

## What came before calculus, and why it was not enough

Before Fermat, finding a maximum or minimum was a bag of ad-hoc tricks — completing the
square for a quadratic, clever inequalities, geometric arguments specific to one curve.
There was no *general* method: change the function and you started over.

Around 1636, Pierre de Fermat noticed something that did generalize. Near a smooth peak
or trough, the function is *almost flat*: nudging the input barely changes the output,
because the change of output is (to first order) proportional to the slope, and the slope
is dying to zero. Fermat turned this into his method of *adequality* — set the
first-order change to zero. A few decades later Newton and Leibniz gave that nudge a name,
the **derivative**, and Fermat's observation became the rule we still teach:
**extrema of a smooth function occur where the derivative is zero.** That is the *why*
behind $f'(x)=0$, and we are going to derive it rather than recite it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is 'set the derivative to zero' a definition, a theorem, or a heuristic?"* It is a
**theorem with hypotheses**, and naming the hypotheses is the whole point of this
article. The precise statement is **Fermat's stationary-point theorem**: *if $f$ is
differentiable at an interior point $x^\star$ of its domain and has a local extremum
there, then $f'(x^\star)=0$.* The three load-bearing words are **differentiable**
(no kinks — see @sec-nondiff), **interior** (not at a boundary — see @sec-boundary), and
**local extremum** (the converse fails — a zero derivative need not be an extremum, see
@sec-second-order). An interviewer who asks this is checking whether you know it is
conditional, not magic.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture standing on the graph of $f$, the height being the value you want to minimize.
You can only move left or right. **The question "am I at the lowest point?" is local: it
is answered entirely by the ground right under your feet.**

If the ground slopes — say it tilts down to the right — then taking a small step right
*lowers* you. You were not at the bottom. The only way no small step can lower you is if
the ground is **level**: the tangent line is horizontal. That is the entire content of
$f'(x)=0$, stated before any algebra. @fig-downhill makes it concrete.

```{python}
#| label: fig-downhill
#| fig-cap: "Why a nonzero slope means you are not at the bottom. At the left point the tangent (red) tilts downward to the right, so a small step right strictly lowers $f$ — there is a downhill direction, so this cannot be a minimum. At the bottom (green) the tangent is flat: no small step in either direction lowers $f$. 'Minimum' and 'flat tangent' are the same statement seen two ways."
f  = lambda x: 0.25 * (x - 3.0) ** 2 + 1.0
fp = lambda x: 0.5 * (x - 3.0)
xs = np.linspace(-0.5, 6.5, 400)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)$")

# a point with nonzero slope (downhill to the right)
x0 = 0.6
ax.scatter([x0], [f(x0)], color=TANGENT, s=55, zorder=4)
tx = np.linspace(x0 - 1.1, x0 + 1.1, 2)
ax.plot(tx, f(x0) + fp(x0) * (tx - x0), color=TANGENT, lw=1.6,
        label="tilted tangent: step right lowers $f$")
ax.annotate("", xy=(x0 + 0.9, f(x0) + fp(x0) * 0.9), xytext=(x0, f(x0)),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=TANGENT, lw=1.8))

# the minimum: flat tangent
ax.scatter([3.0], [f(3.0)], color=ACCENT, s=70, zorder=4)
ax.plot([3.0 - 1.0, 3.0 + 1.0], [f(3.0), f(3.0)], color=ACCENT, lw=1.6,
        label="flat tangent: no downhill direction")

ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("A nonzero slope is a downhill arrow")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Two warnings are already visible in this picture and we will pay both off in full.
First, *flat* is not the same as *lowest*: the top of a hill is flat too, and so is the
momentary pause of an inflection. Second, the argument needed a step in **either**
direction to be available — at the edge of the feasible set one direction is forbidden,
and a slope can persist at the true minimum. Hold those two thoughts.

# The first-order condition, derived {#sec-first-order}

We now turn the picture into mathematics, deriving $f'(x)=0$ from the definition of the
derivative — nothing assumed above the floor.

## What the derivative is, in one breath {#sec-deriv-refresher}

The reader has seen this and is rusty, so we restate it exactly once. The **derivative**
of $f$ at $x$ is the limit of the slope of the line through two nearby points as they
collapse together:

$$
f'(x) \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}.
$$ {#eq-deriv-def}

Read it as a rate: $f'(x)$ is *how fast the output changes per unit change in the input,
right at $x$*. Its sign is the only thing we need for the first-order argument. If
$f'(x) > 0$, then for all sufficiently small $h$ the quotient
$\bigl(f(x+h)-f(x)\bigr)/h$ is close to that positive number, hence itself positive; the
output rises as the input rises. If $f'(x) < 0$, the output falls as the input rises.
That is the whole toolkit.

## The interior first-order condition {#sec-fermat}

::: {#thm-fermat}
**Theorem (Fermat's first-order necessary condition).** Let $f$ be differentiable at a
point $x^\star$ that is **interior** to the feasible set $C$ (so a whole open interval
$(x^\star - \delta,\, x^\star + \delta)$ lies inside $C$). If $x^\star$ is a **local
minimum** of $f$ on $C$ — meaning $f(x^\star) \le f(x)$ for every $x$ within some small
distance — then

$$
f'(x^\star) = 0.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We argue by contradiction: suppose $f'(x^\star) \neq 0$ and show $x^\star$ cannot be a
local minimum. There are two cases.

**Case 1: $f'(x^\star) > 0$.** By the definition @eq-deriv-def, the difference quotient
$\frac{f(x^\star + h) - f(x^\star)}{h}$ tends to a *positive* limit as $h \to 0$. By the
definition of a limit, there is a small $\delta_1 > 0$ such that for every nonzero $h$
with $|h| < \delta_1$, the quotient is still positive (it is within, say, half of
$f'(x^\star)$ of that positive value, hence above zero). Now take a *negative* step: pick
$h$ with $-\delta_1 < h < 0$. The quotient is positive and its denominator $h$ is
negative, so the numerator must be negative:

$$
\frac{f(x^\star + h) - f(x^\star)}{h} > 0
\quad\text{and}\quad h < 0
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
f(x^\star + h) - f(x^\star) < 0,
$$

that is, $f(x^\star + h) < f(x^\star)$. We found a nearby point with a *smaller* value, to
the **left** of $x^\star$. Because $x^\star$ is interior, this left neighbor is genuinely
inside $C$ and allowed. So $x^\star$ is not a local minimum — contradiction.

**Case 2: $f'(x^\star) < 0$.** Mirror image. The quotient is negative for all small
$|h| < \delta_2$. Take a *positive* step, $0 < h < \delta_2$: a negative quotient with a
positive denominator forces a negative numerator, $f(x^\star + h) - f(x^\star) < 0$, so
$f(x^\star + h) < f(x^\star)$. A smaller value sits to the **right**, again inside $C$ by
interiority. Contradiction once more.

Both cases of $f'(x^\star) \neq 0$ produce a strictly lower nearby feasible point, so they
are impossible at a local minimum. The only remaining possibility is $f'(x^\star) = 0$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That proof is the rigorous version of the downhill-arrow picture: *a nonzero derivative is
a guaranteed direction of decrease* (left if the slope is positive, right if it is
negative), and a guaranteed direction of decrease is incompatible with being at the
bottom. Notice the proof never used that $x^\star$ was a *minimum* specifically — the same
argument at a *maximum* finds a higher point — so the condition $f'=0$ catches **all**
interior extrema, minima and maxima alike. That double duty is exactly why $f'=0$ alone
cannot tell a minimum from a maximum, and why we need the second-order test of
@sec-second-order.

::: {.callout-important title="The word that does the work: *stationary*"}
A point where $f'(x)=0$ is called a **stationary point** (or **critical point**). The
name is apt: to first order, $f$ is *stationary* — momentarily not changing — as you pass
through. @thm-fermat says **every interior local extremum is a stationary point.** It does
**not** say the reverse. Stationarity is the *net* we cast; @sec-second-order is how we
sort the catch. This necessary-but-not-sufficient pattern is the spine of the whole
Optimization track: $\nabla f = 0$ in [OPTM-02], the KKT conditions in [OPTM-14] — all of
them are "stationarity nets" followed by a second-order or convexity check.
:::

# Sorting the catch: the second-derivative test {#sec-second-order}

Solving $f'(x)=0$ hands us a list of stationary points. Some are minima, some maxima, some
neither. We need a *local* test to classify each one without plotting the whole function.

## The intuition: curvature {#sec-curvature}

At a stationary point the slope is zero, so the slope is *not* informative — it is the
same (zero) at a min and a max. What distinguishes them is **how the slope is changing**,
i.e. the slope of the slope: the **second derivative** $f''$.

- At a minimum, the curve bends *upward* like a bowl: just left of the bottom the slope is
  negative (going down), just right it is positive (going up). The slope is **increasing**
  through the stationary point, so $f'' > 0$.
- At a maximum, the curve bends *downward* like a dome: the slope goes from positive to
  negative, **decreasing**, so $f'' < 0$.

That is the whole idea. @fig-curvature shows both, plus the treacherous in-between case.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curvature
#| fig-cap: "The second derivative reads curvature. **Left:** $f(x)=x^2$ curves upward ($f''=2>0$) — its stationary point at $0$ is a minimum. **Middle:** $f(x)=-x^2$ curves downward ($f''=-2<0$) — a maximum. **Right:** $f(x)=x^3$ has $f'(0)=0$ *and* $f''(0)=0$; it is neither a min nor a max but an inflection where the curve crosses its own tangent. A zero second derivative is the test going silent, not a verdict."
xs = np.linspace(-1.6, 1.6, 400)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.4))

for ax, (g, ttl, sub) in zip(axes, [
    (lambda x: x**2,  "$f(x)=x^2$",  "$f''(0)=2>0$: minimum"),
    (lambda x: -x**2, "$f(x)=-x^2$", "$f''(0)=-2<0$: maximum"),
    (lambda x: x**3,  "$f(x)=x^3$",  "$f''(0)=0$: inconclusive"),
]):
    ax.plot(xs, g(xs), color=CURVE)
    ax.scatter([0], [g(0.0)], color=ACCENT, s=60, zorder=4)
    ax.axhline(g(0.0), color=GREY, lw=1.0, ls="--", alpha=0.7)  # flat tangent at 0
    ax.set_title(ttl, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlabel(sub, fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The test, stated and proved {#sec-sot-proof}

::: {#thm-second-order}
**Theorem (second-derivative test).** Let $f$ be twice differentiable near a stationary
point $x^\star$ (so $f'(x^\star)=0$), with $f''$ continuous there. Then:

1. if $f''(x^\star) > 0$, the point $x^\star$ is a **strict local minimum**;
2. if $f''(x^\star) < 0$, the point $x^\star$ is a **strict local maximum**;
3. if $f''(x^\star) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** — $x^\star$ may be a minimum, a
   maximum, or neither.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove (1); (2) is the identical argument applied to $-f$, and we settle (3) by explicit
counterexamples below.

Assume $f''(x^\star) > 0$. The second derivative is the *rate of change of the first
derivative*: $f''(x^\star) = \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f'(x^\star + h) - f'(x^\star)}{h}$. Since
$f'(x^\star)=0$, this reads $f''(x^\star) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{f'(x^\star+h)}{h}$, and by
assumption that limit is positive. As in @thm-fermat's proof, a positive limit means there
is a $\delta>0$ such that $\frac{f'(x^\star+h)}{h} > 0$ for all $0 < |h| < \delta$. Reading
off the sign of $f'$ on each side:

- for $0 < h < \delta$ (just to the **right**): the denominator $h$ is positive, so the
  numerator $f'(x^\star + h)$ is positive — $f$ is **increasing** there;
- for $-\delta < h < 0$ (just to the **left**): the denominator is negative, so
  $f'(x^\star + h)$ is negative — $f$ is **decreasing** there.

So $f$ decreases as we approach $x^\star$ from the left and increases as we leave to the
right. Concretely, take any point $x = x^\star + h$ with $0 < |h| < \delta$. We use the
**Mean Value Theorem** — a Calc-1 result: for a function differentiable on an interval,
the total change equals the derivative at *some* interior point times the width,
$f(x) - f(x^\star) = f'(c)\,(x - x^\star)$ for some $c$ strictly between $x^\star$ and $x$.

If $x$ is to the right ($h>0$), then $c \in (x^\star, x)$ lies in the right region where we
just showed $f' > 0$; and $x - x^\star = h > 0$. Product of two positives:
$f(x) - f(x^\star) > 0$. If $x$ is to the left ($h<0$), then $c$ lies in the left region
where $f' < 0$, and $x - x^\star = h < 0$. Product of two negatives:
$f(x) - f(x^\star) > 0$ again. Either way $f(x) > f(x^\star)$ for every nearby $x \neq
x^\star$, which is exactly the definition of a **strict local minimum**.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The proof says in slow motion what @fig-curvature shows: $f'' > 0$ forces the slope to
swing from negative (falling) to positive (rising) as we cross $x^\star$, and a slope that
goes down-then-up traces a valley.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a function where the second-derivative test fails, and say what you'd do
instead."* Reach for $x^4$, $-x^4$, and $x^3$ at the origin — all three have
$f'(0)=f''(0)=0$, yet $x^4$ has a minimum, $-x^4$ a maximum, and $x^3$ an inflection
(neither). The test is genuinely blind here. **What you do instead:** fall back to the
*definition* — check the sign of $f$ on each side of the point, or use the **higher-order
test** (the first nonzero derivative). For $x^4$, the first nonzero derivative at $0$ is
the fourth, $f^{(4)}(0)=24>0$, and the rule is: if the first nonzero derivative is of
**even** order and **positive**, it is a minimum; even and negative, a maximum; **odd**
order, an inflection (neither). We verify this on $x^4$ next.
:::

## The honest $f''=0$ case: $x^4$ worked out {#sec-x4}

Clause (3) of @thm-second-order is not a technicality to wave away; it is a real hole in
the test, and the cleanest way to internalize it is to watch it happen. Take

$$
f(x) = x^4, \qquad f'(x) = 4x^3, \qquad f''(x) = 12x^2.
$$

The stationary points solve $4x^3 = 0$, so $x^\star = 0$ is the only one. Now run the
test: $f''(0) = 12\cdot 0^2 = 0$. **Inconclusive.** The machine has stopped. Yet the
definition settles it directly: $x^4 \ge 0$ for every real $x$, with equality *only* at
$x=0$, so $f(0)=0$ is the smallest value $f$ takes anywhere — $0$ is the global minimum.
The test failed not because the answer is hard but because $x^4$ is *flatter than
quadratic* at the bottom — it curves upward so gently that the second derivative, which
only sees quadratic curvature, reads zero.

The honest resolution is the higher-order test from the callout. Differentiate until a
derivative is nonzero at $0$:

$$
f'(0)=0,\quad f''(0)=0,\quad f'''(x)=24x \Rightarrow f'''(0)=0,\quad f^{(4)}(x)=24
\Rightarrow f^{(4)}(0)=24 \neq 0.
$$

The first nonzero derivative is the **fourth** — even order — and it is **positive**, so
$0$ is a strict local minimum, confirming what the picture screamed. @fig-x4 contrasts the
three flat-at-zero functions so the rule's logic is visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-x4
#| fig-cap: "Three functions that all satisfy $f'(0)=f''(0)=0$, so the second-derivative test is silent on every one. The higher-order test decides: $x^4$ (first nonzero derivative $f^{(4)}=24$, even and positive) is a minimum; $-x^4$ (even, negative) is a maximum; $x^3$ (first nonzero derivative $f'''=6$, odd order) is an inflection — neither. Same blind spot, three different truths."
xs = np.linspace(-1.3, 1.3, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, xs**4,  color=CURVE,  label="$x^4$  (min: even order 4, $+$)")
ax.plot(xs, -xs**4, color=TANGENT, label="$-x^4$ (max: even order 4, $-$)")
ax.plot(xs, xs**3,  color=ACCENT, label="$x^3$  (inflection: odd order 3)")
ax.scatter([0, 0, 0], [0, 0, 0], color="black", s=30, zorder=5)
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("When $f''=0$, the order of the first nonzero derivative decides")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.7)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Worked example 1 — a quadratic cost, by hand {#sec-quadratic}

We start where the method is exact and we can check every number: a quadratic objective,
the bread-and-butter cost of least squares and of the local model that Newton's method
([OPTM-09]) builds everywhere.

A factory's per-unit cost as a function of the production rate $x$ (units per hour) is well
modeled by an upward-opening quadratic — costs are high when you run too slow (fixed
overhead spread thin) and high when you run too fast (overtime, waste), with a sweet spot
between. Suppose

$$
f(x) = 2x^2 - 16x + 40, \qquad x > 0.
$$ {#eq-quad}

**Step 1 — first-order condition.** Differentiate with the Calc-1 power rule
($\frac{d}{dx}x^n = n x^{n-1}$, and the derivative of a constant is $0$):

$$
f'(x) = 4x - 16.
$$

Set it to zero (@thm-fermat) and solve the linear equation:

$$
4x - 16 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^\star = 4.
$$

**Step 2 — second-order check.** Differentiate again: $f''(x) = 4$, a positive constant.
Since $f''(4) = 4 > 0$, by @thm-second-order clause (1) the stationary point $x^\star = 4$
is a strict local minimum. For this quadratic the local minimum is in fact the **global**
minimum, and we can see exactly why by completing the square rather than appealing to any
later result. Factor out the leading $2$ and complete the square inside:

$$
f(x) = 2x^2 - 16x + 40 = 2\bigl(x^2 - 8x\bigr) + 40
     = 2\bigl((x-4)^2 - 16\bigr) + 40 = 2(x-4)^2 + 8.
$$

The term $2(x-4)^2$ is a square times a positive constant, so it is $\ge 0$ for every $x$
and equals $0$ *only* at $x=4$. Hence $f(x) \ge 8$ everywhere, with equality only at
$x=4$: the stationary point is the global minimizer, no qualification needed. (This
"$f''>0$ everywhere $\Rightarrow$ a single global minimum" pattern is the seed of
**convexity**, the dividing line of the field, developed in general in [OPTM-04]; for a
quadratic the completed square proves it outright.)

**Step 3 — the value.** The minimum *cost* is $f(4) = 2(16) - 16(4) + 40 = 32 - 64 + 40 =
8$. So the cheapest run rate is $4$ units/hour at a cost of $8$.

@fig-quadratic confirms the hand arithmetic and overlays the flat tangent that
$f'(4)=0$ guarantees.

```{python}
#| label: fig-quadratic
#| fig-cap: "Worked example 1. The quadratic cost $f(x)=2x^2-16x+40$ with its unique stationary point at $x^\\star=4$ (green), where the tangent is flat ($f'(4)=0$) and the curve bowls upward ($f''=4>0$), so it is the global minimum. The closed-form answer and the picture agree to the last digit."
f  = lambda x: 2*x**2 - 16*x + 40
fp = lambda x: 4*x - 16
xs = np.linspace(0, 8, 400)
xstar = 4.0

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)=2x^2-16x+40$")
ax.scatter([xstar], [f(xstar)], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=5,
           label=r"$x^\star=4,\ f=8$")
ax.plot([xstar-1.6, xstar+1.6], [f(xstar), f(xstar)], color=TANGENT, lw=1.6,
        label="flat tangent ($f'=0$)")
ax.set_xlabel("production rate $x$ (units/hr)"); ax.set_ylabel("cost $f(x)$")
ax.set_title("A quadratic cost: $f'(x)=0$ is exact here")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For a general quadratic $f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$, where is the minimum, and when does one even
exist?"* Differentiate: $f'(x) = 2ax + b$, set to zero, $x^\star = -\,b/(2a)$ — the
vertex formula, now *derived* rather than memorized. The second derivative is $f''=2a$, so
it is a **minimum iff $a>0$**, a maximum iff $a<0$, and if $a=0$ the function is a straight
line with no interior extremum at all (it just runs off to $\pm\infty$). This is the
simplest instance of the rule that governs Newton's method: every smooth function looks
quadratic up close, and the sign of the curvature decides everything.
:::

# Worked example 2 — a one-parameter negative log-likelihood {#sec-poisson}

The previous example was algebra. This one is the form practitioners meet constantly:
**maximizing a likelihood by minimizing its negative logarithm**, with a single parameter.
It is also where "set the derivative to zero" earns its keep statistically.

A call center logs the number of calls arriving each minute. Counts of independent
events in a fixed window are the textbook home of the **Poisson distribution**: a count
$y$ has probability $p(y \mid \lambda) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{y}/y!$, where the single
parameter $\lambda > 0$ is the *mean rate* — the average number of calls per minute. We
observe $n$ minutes with counts $y_1,\dots,y_n$ and want the rate $\lambda$ that best
explains them. (The Poisson model itself is built in [PROB-12]; here we need only its
formula and treat finding $\lambda$ as the one-dimensional optimization it is.)

**Step 1 — write the objective.** The **likelihood** is the probability of all the data as
a function of $\lambda$; by independence it is the product of the per-minute
probabilities. Maximizing a product of small numbers is numerically and algebraically
nasty, so we take the negative logarithm and **minimize** that instead — legitimate
because $-\ln$ is strictly decreasing, so it flips "most likely" into "smallest," moving
the maximizer to the identical location (the same log trick the OLS article used in
[REGR-01]). Writing the objective and expanding with $\ln(ab)=\ln a + \ln b$ and
$\ln e^{z}=z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(\lambda)
&= -\ln \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{\,y_i}}{y_i!}
 = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Bigl[\,\ln e^{-\lambda} + \ln \lambda^{\,y_i} - \ln(y_i!)\,\Bigr] \\[4pt]
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Bigl[\,-\lambda + y_i \ln\lambda - \ln(y_i!)\,\Bigr]
 = n\lambda - \Bigl(\textstyle\sum_i y_i\Bigr)\ln\lambda + \sum_i \ln(y_i!).
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-nll}

**Step 2 — first-order condition.** Differentiate @eq-nll with respect to $\lambda$. The
last term has no $\lambda$ (it is a constant, derivative $0$); the derivative of $n\lambda$
is $n$; and $\frac{d}{d\lambda}\ln\lambda = 1/\lambda$ (a Calc-1 fact). So

$$
\ell'(\lambda) = n - \frac{\sum_i y_i}{\lambda}.
$$

Set $\ell'(\lambda)=0$ and solve:

$$
n - \frac{\sum_i y_i}{\lambda} = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\lambda = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = \bar y.
$$

The rate that best explains the data is simply the **sample mean** of the counts — a
clean, interpretable answer that the derivative test produced mechanically.

**Step 3 — second-order check.** Differentiate again. With
$\ell'(\lambda) = n - (\sum_i y_i)\lambda^{-1}$ and
$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\lambda^{-1} = -\lambda^{-2}$,

$$
\ell''(\lambda) = \frac{\sum_i y_i}{\lambda^2}.
$$

For any data with at least one nonzero count, $\sum_i y_i > 0$ and $\lambda^2 > 0$, so
$\ell''(\bar y) > 0$: a strict minimum by @thm-second-order, and since $\ell''>0$ for all
$\lambda>0$ it is the unique global minimizer on the feasible set $\lambda > 0$.

@fig-poisson draws the negative log-likelihood for a simulated day and marks the analytic
minimizer $\bar y$, confirming it sits at the bottom.

```{python}
#| label: fig-poisson
#| fig-cap: "Worked example 2. The Poisson negative log-likelihood $\\ell(\\lambda)$ for $n=200$ simulated minutes of call counts. The curve is a smooth bowl in the rate $\\lambda$; its unique minimizer (green) is the closed-form $\\hat\\lambda=\\bar y$ that solving $\\ell'(\\lambda)=0$ produced, marked here against the bottom located by a brute-force grid search; the printed output below shows the two agree to several decimals. The derivative test turns a probability model into a one-line estimator."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
true_lambda = 4.3
y = rng.poisson(true_lambda, size=200)
n = y.size
ybar = y.mean()

# negative log-likelihood up to the data-only constant sum ln(y_i!)
const = np.sum([np.sum(np.log(np.arange(1, yi + 1))) for yi in y])
def nll(lam):
    return n * lam - y.sum() * np.log(lam) + const

lams = np.linspace(2.5, 6.5, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(lams, nll(lams), color=CURVE, label=r"$\ell(\lambda)$")
ax.scatter([ybar], [nll(ybar)], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=5,
           label=fr"$\hat\lambda=\bar y={ybar:.3f}$")
ax.axvline(ybar, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls="--", alpha=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel(r"rate $\lambda$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"negative log-likelihood $\ell(\lambda)$")
ax.set_title("Minimizing a 1-parameter negative log-likelihood")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"closed-form minimizer  ybar      = {ybar:.4f}")
print(f"numerical argmin over grid        = {lams[np.argmin(nll(lams))]:.4f}")
print(f"second derivative at ybar > 0?    = {y.sum()/ybar**2:.3f}  (>0, confirms minimum)")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You set the derivative of the log-likelihood to zero — but you were minimizing the
*negative* log-likelihood. Doesn't the sign matter?"* It does not change the **location**,
and that is the point worth being crisp about. Maximizing $\ln L$ and minimizing
$-\ln L$ are the same problem because negation is strictly decreasing: it flips the curve
top-to-bottom, sending the highest point to the lowest *at the same $\lambda$*. The
stationarity equation is literally identical up to an overall sign,
$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\ln L = -\,\frac{d}{d\lambda}(-\ln L)$, so setting either to zero gives
the same $\hat\lambda$. The **maximize-vs-minimize** distinction is pure convention; the
*second-order* sign is what actually flips (a max of $\ln L$ is a min of $-\ln L$). This is
the same gradient-ascent/descent mirror that [OPTM-06] makes explicit in many dimensions.
:::

# Worked example 3 — the edge of applicability: a boundary minimum {#sec-boundary}

Now the honest case, the one the Core warned about and the reason this article is not just
"set $f'=0$ and stop." Here the derivative test, applied naively, gives the **wrong
answer** — and a practitioner who trusts it blindly ships a bad decision.

A retailer chooses an order quantity $x \ge 0$ for a perishable good. The total cost
combines a holding/overage cost that grows with $x$ and a shortage penalty that shrinks
with $x$, but a supplier contract caps the order at $x \le 6$. A realistic shape is

$$
f(x) = (x - 9)^2, \qquad \text{feasible set } C = [\,0,\, 6\,].
$$ {#eq-boundary}

The *unconstrained* parabola is minimized at $x=9$ — but $9$ is **outside** the feasible
interval; the contract forbids it. Run the derivative test as taught and watch it
mislead.

**The naive (wrong) move.** Set $f'(x)=0$: $f'(x) = 2(x-9) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 9$. This is
the only stationary point, and $f''=2>0$ certifies it as a minimum. A reader who stops
here reports "minimum at $x=9$." But $9 \notin C$ — the answer is not even *allowed*.
@thm-fermat does not apply, because its hypothesis demanded an **interior** point, and the
true optimum is nowhere near the interior.

**The correct procedure on a closed interval.** When the feasible set is a closed interval
$[a,b]$, a continuous $f$ attains its minimum somewhere on it (this existence guarantee is
OPTM-00's, the "closed and bounded" fact). The minimizer is at one of only three kinds of
place, and we simply check them all — the standard Calc-1 *closed-interval method*:

1. **interior stationary points** (where $f'=0$ *and* the point is inside $(a,b)$),
2. the **left endpoint** $a$,
3. the **right endpoint** $b$.

For @eq-boundary: the only stationary point $x=9$ is **not** in $(0,6)$, so it is
discarded. That leaves the two endpoints. Evaluate:

$$
f(0) = (0-9)^2 = 81, \qquad f(6) = (6-9)^2 = 9.
$$

The smallest feasible value is $f(6) = 9$, so the constrained minimizer is the **boundary
point** $x^\star = 6$. Crucially, the derivative there is *not* zero:
$f'(6) = 2(6-9) = -6 \neq 0$. The function is still sloping downhill toward $9$ at the
boundary; we simply are not allowed to keep walking. @fig-boundary shows the parabola, the
forbidden vertex, and the true optimum pinned against the wall.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boundary
#| fig-cap: "Worked example 3, the edge of applicability. The cost $f(x)=(x-9)^2$ is minimized over the *feasible* interval $[0,6]$ (shaded). The unconstrained vertex $x=9$ (red, hollow) satisfies $f'=0$ but is forbidden. The true constrained minimum (green) is the **boundary point** $x^\\star=6$, where the slope is still nonzero ($f'(6)=-6$): the function wants to keep decreasing but the wall stops it. Here 'set the derivative to zero' gives the wrong answer outright."
f  = lambda x: (x - 9)**2
fp = lambda x: 2*(x - 9)
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 400)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axvspan(0, 6, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.08, label="feasible set $[0,6]$")
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)=(x-9)^2$")
# forbidden unconstrained minimizer
ax.scatter([9], [f(9)], facecolors="none", edgecolors=TANGENT, s=90, linewidth=2,
           zorder=5, label=r"unconstrained $f'=0$ at $x=9$ (forbidden)")
# true constrained minimizer at the boundary
ax.scatter([6], [f(6)], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=6,
           label=r"constrained min at boundary $x^\star=6$")
# show the nonzero slope at the boundary
tx = np.linspace(6 - 1.3, 6 + 1.3, 2)
ax.plot(tx, f(6) + fp(6) * (tx - 6), color=TANGENT, lw=1.5,
        label=r"tangent at $x=6$: slope $-6\neq 0$")
ax.axvline(6, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel("order quantity $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("cost $f(x)$")
ax.set_title("A minimum at the boundary, where $f'\\neq 0$")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**"Set $f'(x)=0$" finds only *interior* stationary points. It is blind to the boundary.**
On a constrained interval $[a,b]$ you must *also* evaluate the endpoints and compare,
because the optimum can sit on a wall with a nonzero slope. Forgetting this is one of the
most common optimization mistakes in practice — and it is the entire reason constrained
optimization needs its own machinery. The endpoint comparison here is the one-dimensional
ancestor of the **KKT conditions** ([OPTM-14]): an *active* constraint (a binding wall)
contributes a nonzero multiplier precisely because the unconstrained slope there is not
zero. When you next see complementary slackness, remember $f'(6)=-6$.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The derivative test also presumes differentiability.** A function with a *kink* — like
$f(x)=|x|$, whose minimum is the corner at $0$ — has no derivative at its minimizer, so
$f'(x)=0$ never holds there even though the minimum is interior. @sec-nondiff treats this;
the general fix, the **subgradient**, is owned by [OPTM-05] and is what lets the lasso be
optimized at its kinks.
:::

# When you cannot solve $f'(x)=0$: the first iterative search {#sec-iterative}

Every example so far had a tidy closed form: $f'(x)=0$ was a linear or simple equation we
solved with algebra. That is the lucky case. In general $f'(x)=0$ is **transcendental** —
no formula isolates $x$. Try minimizing $f(x) = x - 2\ln x + \tfrac{1}{10}e^{x/3}$: the
condition $f'(x) = 1 - 2/x + \tfrac{1}{30}e^{x/3} = 0$ tangles a polynomial term, a
reciprocal, and an exponential, and no rearrangement frees $x$. We cannot *solve*; we must
**search**. This is the conceptual birth of every iterative optimizer in the rest of the
track.

The key enabling idea is **unimodality**: a function is unimodal on $[a,b]$ if it
decreases to a single low point and then increases — one valley, no false bottoms. (Every
strictly convex function is unimodal; we will not need full convexity here.) Unimodality
lets us *shrink* the interval that must contain the minimum, without ever solving an
equation.

## Bisection on the derivative {#sec-bisection}

If $f$ is unimodal and differentiable, its derivative $f'$ does something clean: it is
**negative** while $f$ is still falling (left of the minimum) and **positive** once $f$ is
rising (right of it), crossing zero exactly at $x^\star$. So locating the minimum of $f$ is
the same as locating the **sign change of $f'$** — and finding a sign change is what
bisection does.

The method: keep an interval $[a,b]$ with $f'(a) < 0$ and $f'(b) > 0$ (so the crossing is
trapped inside). Evaluate the derivative at the midpoint $m = (a+b)/2$:

- if $f'(m) > 0$, the crossing is to the **left**, so replace $b \leftarrow m$;
- if $f'(m) < 0$, the crossing is to the **right**, so replace $a \leftarrow m$;
- if $f'(m) = 0$, you landed on it exactly — stop.

Each step **halves** the interval, so after $k$ steps the bracket has width
$(b-a)/2^{k}$ — the uncertainty shrinks by a constant factor every iteration. This is the
first *convergence rate* in the series: error $\propto 2^{-k}$, called **linear**
convergence (linear in the log of the error). @fig-bisection traces it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bisection
#| fig-cap: "Bisection on $f'$ for the transcendental objective $f(x)=x-2\\ln x+\\tfrac{1}{10}e^{x/3}$, whose $f'(x)=0$ has no closed form. **Left:** $f$ with the true minimizer (green). **Right:** the bracket $[a,b]$ trapping the sign change of $f'$, halving every iteration (rows top to bottom). After a handful of steps the interval is microscopic — convergence is geometric, error $\\propto 2^{-k}$."
f  = lambda x: x - 2*np.log(x) + 0.1*np.exp(x/3)
fp = lambda x: 1 - 2/x + (1/30)*np.exp(x/3)

a, b = 0.5, 6.0
assert fp(a) < 0 and fp(b) > 0
brackets = [(a, b)]
for _ in range(8):
    m = 0.5*(a+b)
    if fp(m) > 0:
        b = m
    else:
        a = m
    brackets.append((a, b))
xstar = 0.5*(a+b)

xs = np.linspace(0.4, 6.2, 400)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)$")
axL.scatter([xstar], [f(xstar)], color=ACCENT, s=70, zorder=5,
            label=fr"$x^\star\approx{xstar:.4f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
axL.set_title("A transcendental objective"); axL.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9)

for k, (lo, hi) in enumerate(brackets):
    axR.plot([lo, hi], [k, k], color=GREY, lw=2.0, marker="|", markersize=9)
axR.axvline(xstar, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="true minimizer")
axR.invert_yaxis()
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("iteration $k$")
axR.set_title("Bracket halves each step"); axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"final bracket width = {b-a:.2e} after 8 steps  (started at {6.0-0.5:.1f})")
print(f"estimated minimizer = {xstar:.6f}")
```

## Golden-section search: when you only have $f$, not $f'$ {#sec-golden}

Bisection needed the **derivative**. Sometimes you cannot get it — $f$ is a black box (a
simulation, an experiment, a non-differentiable score) and all you can do is *evaluate*
it. **Golden-section search** minimizes a unimodal $f$ using function values alone, by
shrinking a bracket $[a,b]$ known to contain the minimum.

Place two interior probes $c < d$ inside $[a,b]$ and compare:

- if $f(c) < f(d)$, the minimum cannot be to the right of $d$ (unimodality: past the
  valley $f$ only rises), so discard $(d, b]$ — set $b \leftarrow d$;
- if $f(c) > f(d)$, symmetrically discard $[a, c)$ — set $a \leftarrow c$.

The clever part is *where* to place $c, d$ so that one probe can be **reused** next round,
saving an evaluation. Let us *derive* the placement rather than assert it. Work on a unit
bracket $[0,1]$ (any interval rescales to this), place the two probes symmetrically a
fraction $\rho \in (0, \tfrac12)$ in from each end,

$$
c = \rho, \qquad d = 1 - \rho,
$$

and suppose the comparison discards the right piece, so the new bracket becomes
$[0,\,1-\rho]$ of width $1-\rho$. The old left probe $c=\rho$ survives inside this new
bracket. For the *next* round to reuse it, $c$ must already sit where a freshly placed
probe would go — namely a fraction $\rho$ in from one end of $[0,\,1-\rho]$. Measured from
the **right** end $1-\rho$, a fresh probe lands at $(1-\rho) - \rho(1-\rho) = (1-\rho)^2$.
Setting the survivor's position equal to that target,

$$
\rho = (1-\rho)^2 = 1 - 2\rho + \rho^2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \rho^2 - 3\rho + 1 = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \rho = \frac{3 - \sqrt5}{2} \approx 0.382,
$$

taking the root in $(0,\tfrac12)$ by the quadratic formula (the other root,
$\tfrac{3+\sqrt5}{2}\approx 2.618$, is out of range). The *surviving* probe then sits a
fraction $1-\rho = \tfrac{\sqrt5 - 1}{2} = 1/\varphi \approx 0.618$ in from the near end,
where $\varphi = (1+\sqrt5)/2 \approx 1.618$ is the **golden ratio** — the reuse
requirement has *forced* the golden ratio out of a quadratic, with no appeal to any
outside fact. With the probes placed this way, each iteration shrinks the bracket by
exactly the constant factor $1-\rho = 1/\varphi \approx 0.618$ — again **linear**
convergence, slightly slower per step than bisection's $1/2$, but it never touches a
derivative. @fig-golden runs it on the same objective.

```{python}
#| label: fig-golden
#| fig-cap: "Golden-section search on the same $f$, using *function values only* (no derivative). Two probes split the bracket at the golden ratio so one is reused each round; the bracket shrinks by the constant factor $1/\\varphi\\approx0.618$ every step. Slower per step than bisection but derivative-free — the tool of choice when $f$ is a black box. The bracket (rows top to bottom) closes in on the same minimizer."
f = lambda x: x - 2*np.log(x) + 0.1*np.exp(x/3)
invphi  = (np.sqrt(5) - 1) / 2          # 1/phi  ~ 0.618
invphi2 = (3 - np.sqrt(5)) / 2          # 1/phi^2

a, b = 0.5, 6.0
c = a + invphi2 * (b - a)
d = a + invphi  * (b - a)
fc, fd = f(c), f(d)
gbrackets = [(a, b)]
for _ in range(12):
    if fc < fd:
        b, d, fd = d, c, fc
        c = a + invphi2 * (b - a); fc = f(c)
    else:
        a, c, fc = c, d, fd
        d = a + invphi * (b - a); fd = f(d)
    gbrackets.append((a, b))
xstar_g = 0.5*(a+b)

xs = np.linspace(0.4, 6.2, 400)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)$ (black box)")
axL.scatter([xstar_g], [f(xstar_g)], color=ACCENT, s=70, zorder=5,
            label=fr"$x^\star\approx{xstar_g:.4f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
axL.set_title("Derivative-free objective"); axL.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9)

for k, (lo, hi) in enumerate(gbrackets):
    axR.plot([lo, hi], [k, k], color=GREY, lw=2.0, marker="|", markersize=9)
axR.axvline(xstar_g, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="true minimizer")
axR.invert_yaxis()
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("iteration $k$")
axR.set_title(r"Bracket $\times\,0.618$ each step"); axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"golden-section minimizer = {xstar_g:.6f}  (matches bisection)")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Bisection vs. golden-section — when do you use which, and what is the catch with both?"*
Use **bisection on $f'$** when you can compute the derivative cheaply: it halves the
bracket each step ($\times 0.5$), the fastest of the two. Use **golden-section** when $f$
is a black box with no usable derivative — it needs only function values, at the cost of a
slightly slower $\times 0.618$ shrink and one extra evaluation to set up. **The catch they
share is unimodality.** Both assume a single valley; on a multi-modal function they will
happily converge to *whichever* local minimum their starting bracket traps, with no warning
that a deeper one exists elsewhere. Guaranteeing the local minimum is the global one is
exactly what **convexity** buys you, which is why [OPTM-04] is the dividing line of the
whole field. These bracketing methods are also the engine inside **line search**
([OPTM-07]): every gradient-descent step solves a 1-D "how far do I go?" subproblem this
way.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest boundary {#sec-failure}

We have already met two of the three ways the derivative test fails; collect all of them
here, because knowing the failure surface is the difference between "can take a
derivative" and "knows when to trust it."

## Stationary but not a minimum {#sec-not-min}

The most basic trap, proved structurally in @thm-fermat: $f'(x)=0$ catches maxima and
inflections too. Reporting a stationary point as "the minimum" without the second-order
(or higher-order) check is simply wrong. The fix is cheap and mandatory: always run
@thm-second-order, and fall back to the higher-order test of @sec-x4 when $f''$ vanishes.

## Boundary minima {#sec-boundary-recap}

@sec-boundary in full: on a constrained set the optimum may sit on a wall with
$f' \neq 0$, invisible to "set the derivative to zero." The fix is the closed-interval
method — check interior stationary points **and** endpoints and compare. This is the
gateway to constrained optimization ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-16]).

## Non-differentiable points {#sec-nondiff}

@thm-fermat assumed differentiability. A kink breaks it. The cleanest witness is
$f(x)=|x|$: its minimum is the corner at $x=0$, an interior point of $\mathbb{R}$, yet
$f'(0)$ does not exist — the left slope is $-1$, the right slope is $+1$, and they disagree,
so no single tangent and no "$f'(0)=0$." The derivative test cannot even be *stated* here.
@fig-nondiff shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-nondiff
#| fig-cap: "The derivative test cannot see a kink. $f(x)=|x|$ has its minimum at the corner $x=0$, but $f'(0)$ does not exist — the slope jumps from $-1$ to $+1$ across the point, shown by the two one-sided tangents. No flat tangent exists at the minimizer, so '$f'=0$' has nothing to say. The repair is the *subgradient* (the whole interval of slopes $[-1,1]$ that fit under the graph at $0$), owned by OPTM-05."
xs = np.linspace(-2, 2, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, np.abs(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)=|x|$")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=5, label="minimum at the kink $x=0$")
ax.plot([-1.6, 0], [1.6, 0], color=TANGENT, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="left slope $-1$")
ax.plot([0, 1.6], [0, 1.6], color=GREY,    lw=1.4, ls="--", label="right slope $+1$")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title(r"No derivative at the minimizer: $f'(0)$ undefined")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 2.1)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The honest resolution is not in this article: it is the **subgradient**, which replaces the
single slope at a kink with the *set* of all slopes whose lines stay below the graph (for
$|x|$ at $0$, the whole interval $[-1,1]$), and the optimality condition $0 \in \partial
f(x)$. That machinery is owned by [OPTM-05] and is exactly what makes the lasso solvable.
Here we simply flag the boundary: smooth $\Rightarrow$ use the derivative test; kinked
$\Rightarrow$ you need subgradients.

## Many valleys: local vs. global {#sec-multimodal}

The deepest limitation, and the one the entire rest of the track is organized around. The
derivative test — and the bracketing searches of @sec-iterative — are **local**: they
report a point with no downhill neighbors, but they cannot tell whether a *deeper* valley
exists somewhere else. @fig-multimodal shows a function with several stationary points, two
of which are genuine local minima by the second-derivative test, only one of which is the
global minimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-multimodal
#| fig-cap: "The honest boundary of *local* optimality. This multi-modal $f$ has three stationary points; the second-derivative test certifies two of them as local minima (orange), but only one is the **global** minimum (green). $f'(x)=0$ and any local search are blind to the difference — started in the wrong basin they converge to the wrong valley with full confidence. Ruling this out requires *convexity* (one valley, guaranteed), the subject of OPTM-04."
f  = lambda x: 0.10*x**4 - 0.30*x**3 - 1.6*x**2 + 2.0*x + 12
fp = lambda x: 0.40*x**3 - 0.90*x**2 - 3.2*x + 2.0
fpp = lambda x: 1.20*x**2 - 1.80*x - 3.2
xs = np.linspace(-4.5, 6.0, 600)

# locate stationary points by sign changes of fp on a fine grid
g = np.linspace(-4.5, 6.0, 4000)
sgn = np.sign(fp(g))
roots = g[:-1][np.diff(sgn) != 0]
mins = [r for r in roots if fpp(r) > 0]
gmin = min(mins, key=f)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label="$f(x)$")
for r in mins:
    ax.scatter([r], [f(r)], color="#e08a1e", s=55, zorder=4)
ax.scatter([gmin], [f(gmin)], color=ACCENT, s=95, zorder=6,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.3, label="global minimum")
ax.scatter([], [], color="#e08a1e", s=55, label="local minima ($f''>0$)")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("Local search cannot see past its own valley")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Your optimizer returned a stationary point. How do you know it's the global minimum?"*
The honest answer in one dimension: **in general, you don't** — $f'(x)=0$ and any local
search certify only that no *nearby* point is lower. You earn a global guarantee in exactly
two ways. **(1) Convexity:** if $f$ is convex (one bowl, $f''\ge 0$ everywhere in the
smooth case), every local minimum *is* the global minimum — the result that makes
optimization tractable, derived in [OPTM-04]. **(2) Exhaustion:** on a small, bounded
domain you can grid or multi-start to make a deeper basin unlikely, but that is evidence,
not proof. An interviewer asking this is probing whether you respect the local/global gap;
conflating them is the single most expensive mistake in non-convex optimization (training
neural nets, fitting mixtures), where the gap is unavoidable and we settle for a *good*
local minimum.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You would rarely hand-code
bisection or golden section. Every numerical stack ships robust 1-D minimizers:
`scipy.optimize.minimize_scalar` defaults to **Brent's method** (a hybrid of
golden-section and parabolic interpolation — derivative-free, with golden-section's
guaranteed shrink as a fallback when the parabola misbehaves), and accepts a `bounds=`
argument to honor a feasible interval, automatically handling the endpoint comparison of
@sec-boundary. For the smooth, closed-form cases (@sec-quadratic, @sec-poisson) you would
not optimize numerically at all — you would solve $f'(x)=0$ by hand or with a symbolic tool
(`sympy.solve`) and get the exact answer. The practitioner's habit worth internalizing:
**check whether $f'(x)=0$ has a closed form first** (it often does for the losses in this
series), reach for an iterative scalar minimizer only when it does not, and *always* pass
the bounds when the problem is constrained so the library checks the walls for you.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-00 *What Is an Optimization Problem?*]**, which named the objects
— objective, argmin, feasible set, local vs. global. This article gave the **first method**
for actually finding a minimizer when there is a single variable: $f'(x)=0$, the
second-derivative test, the honest failure clauses, and the seed of iterative search. From
here:

- **[OPTM-02 *Minimizing in Many Variables*]** — the direct sequel. Everything here
  generalizes: the single derivative becomes the **gradient** $\nabla f$, the condition
  $f'(x)=0$ becomes **stationarity** $\nabla f(x)=0$, and the second-derivative test becomes
  the **Hessian test** (positive-definite $\Rightarrow$ minimum, indefinite $\Rightarrow$
  *saddle* — a failure mode with no 1-D analogue). It uses OLS as its headline example, the
  exact move the Regression track links here for.
- **[OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*]** — answers the local-vs-global question (@sec-multimodal):
  for convex $f$, a stationary point *is* the global minimum. This is the guarantee that
  turns "we found a valley" into "we found *the* valley."
- **[OPTM-05 *Subgradients*]** — repairs the non-differentiable case (@sec-nondiff): how to
  do "set the derivative to zero" at a kink like $|x|$.
- **[OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*]** — when there is no closed form *and* many variables, the
  iterative-search idea of @sec-iterative grows into the algorithm that trains nearly every
  model in the series.
- **[OPTM-13 *Lagrange Multipliers*]** and **[OPTM-14 *KKT Conditions*]** — the boundary
  minimum of @sec-boundary, handled properly: the binding-wall, nonzero-slope situation
  becomes an *active constraint* with a nonzero multiplier.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The origin.** Pierre de Fermat's *Methodus ad disquirendam maximam et minimam* (c. 1636)
  introduced setting the first-order change to zero — *adequality* — decades before the
  derivative had a name. It is the direct ancestor of @thm-fermat, which still bears his
  name.
- **The closed-interval method and the second-derivative test** are standard Calculus-I
  material; any rigorous single-variable calculus text (e.g. Spivak's *Calculus*, ch. 11)
  develops them with the Mean Value Theorem we used in @sec-sot-proof.
- **The bracketing searches.** Golden-section search and Brent's method are treated in
  Press et al., *Numerical Recipes*, ch. 10 ("Minimization or Maximization of Functions"),
  and in Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization*, which also develops the line-search
  theory ([OPTM-07]) that these 1-D methods feed.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What if there are many variables?*
  — the gradient, [OPTM-02]. *What if a local minimum is not global?* — convexity,
  [OPTM-04]. *What if $f$ has kinks?* — subgradients, [OPTM-05]. *What if the minimum is at
  a constraint?* — Lagrange/KKT, [OPTM-13]–[OPTM-14]. Each is the multivariate, harder, or
  more general descendant of a failure mode we made explicit in @sec-failure.
